tools.name: list_graph_components
tools.title: List Graph Components
tools.description: Lists published workflow graph components. Filter by category, capability keyword or risk level. Returns id, name, category, capabilities and summary for each match. Use get_graph_component to get full details including related edges.

category.description: Filter by component category: input | processing | state | safety | tool | output | eval | orchestration | integration

capability.description: Filter by capability keyword (case-insensitive substring match on the capabilities list)

risk_level.description: Filter by risk level: low | medium | high | critical

max_results.description: Maximum number of results to return. Default 20.

tools.name: get_graph_component
tools.title: Get Graph Component
tools.description: Returns full details for a single workflow graph component by id. Use include_edges to see related edges (requires, safer_with, produces_input_for, etc.). Use include_tests to see eval criteria and test playbook references.

component_id.description: The id of the component to retrieve (e.g. 'source_retrieval')

include_edges.description: If true, include all incoming and outgoing edges for this component.

include_tests.description: If true, include evals, tested_in_playbooks and tested_in_routes.

tools.name: list_graph_edges
tools.title: List Graph Edges
tools.description: Lists workflow graph edges (relations between components). Filter by source/target component, relation type or severity. Use this to understand what edges exist between components before designing a workflow.

from_component_id.description: Filter edges by source component id

to_component_id.description: Filter edges by target component id

relation.description: Filter by relation type: produces_input_for | requires | safer_with | must_run_before | recommended_for | compatible_with | tested_with | avoid_when | conflicts_with | can_run_parallel

severity.description: Filter by severity level: low | medium | high | critical

max_results.description: Maximum number of results to return. Default 30.

tools.name: get_graph_edge
tools.title: Get Graph Edge
tools.description: Returns full details for a single workflow graph edge by id. Includes the relation type, severity, reason, notes and any conditions. Use list_graph_edges to find edge ids.

edge_id.description: The id of the edge to retrieve (e.g. 'external_publish__requires__human_approval_gate')

tools.name: get_stack_recommendation
tools.title: Get Stack Recommendation
tools.description: Returns the recommended technology stack for an AI workflow, with opinionated defaults and when-to-upgrade guidance. Always warns against premature vector DB, remote auth or graph databases for local/v0 scope. Use this before selecting technologies for a new workflow project.

stack_id.description: Specific stack id to retrieve. Defaults to 'default_orchestratekit_stack'.

use_case.description: Describe your use case to get targeted guidance (e.g. 'local MVP', 'hosted team product')

local_or_hosted.description: Whether you are building a local tool or a hosted product

risk_level.description: Risk level of the workflow to inform stack choices

tools.name: list_known_routes
tools.title: List Known Routes
tools.description: Lists all known golden-path workflow routes in the registry. Each route is a tested sequence of components and edges for a specific workflow pattern. Use get_route to get full details and component/edge lists for a specific route.

tools.name: get_route
tools.title: Get Route
tools.description: Returns full details for a single golden-path workflow route by id. Includes the component list, edge list, confidence score, risk level and failure modes. Use list_known_routes to discover available route ids.

route_id.description: The id of the route to retrieve (e.g. 'research_route_v1')

include_component_details.description: If true, include full component summaries for each component in the route.

tools.name: compose_workflow_route
tools.title: Compose Workflow Route
tools.description: Proposes a candidate AI workflow route by matching your goal to graph components, adding required dependencies and safety gates, ordering steps, and scoring the result. Use this when you are designing a new AI workflow and want opinionated, graph-backed architecture suggestions. The result is a CANDIDATE ROUTE — not a validated playbook. Always review before implementing. Use get_route and list_known_routes to check if a validated playbook already exists.

goal.description: Describe the workflow you want to build in plain language. Example: 'I want to read emails, research the sender and draft a reply.'

must_have_capabilities.description: Capabilities the route must include (e.g. ['send_email', 'validate_schema']). Missing capabilities will be flagged.

must_avoid.description: Component IDs to exclude from the route.

risk_level.description: Maximum acceptable risk level for components. Higher-risk components will be flagged.

local_or_hosted.description: Whether you are building a local tool or a hosted product. Affects stack recommendation.

output_depth.description: guided/brief = short summary only. standard = full route with warnings. technical/deep = includes all evals.

tools.name: get_playbook
tools.title: Get Playbook
tools.description: Returns a golden-path playbook from the workflow graph registry by id or workflow type. Set include_graph=true to also receive the resolved route, component summaries, edge summaries, stack context, untested edges and approval gates. If no playbook matches, recommends compose_workflow_route instead. Returns warnings for beta/deprecated/low-confidence matches. Use list_known_routes to browse available route ids.

playbook_id.description: The exact playbook id to retrieve (e.g. 'codebase_agent_workflow').

workflow_type.description: Match the best golden-path playbook by workflow type (e.g. 'coding-agent', 'research', 'data-pipeline'). Used when playbook_id is not known.

include_beta.description: If true, include beta-status playbooks in the search.

include_graph.description: If true, resolve and return the full graph context: route, components, edges, stack, untested edges and approval gates.

output_format.description: summary = overview only. full = all playbook fields. implementation_focused = guardrails, failure_modes, implementation_steps and graph context.

tools.name: get_relevant_docs
tools.title: Get Relevant Docs
tools.description: Returns documentation and source references relevant to a playbook, route, set of components, or framework/topic from the workflow graph registry and the internal docs-index. Only returns known indexed/internal sources — never fabricates official documentation. Internal seed sources are labelled as 'internal_note'. Use playbook_id or route_id to narrow to a specific workflow. Use frameworks/topics for broad technology-level references.

playbook_id.description: Pull sources from this playbook and its component/edge members.

route_id.description: Pull sources from the edges and components referenced by this route.

component_ids.description: Pull sources from these specific component ids.

frameworks.description: Match docs-index entries by framework tag (e.g. 'openai', 'cursor', 'anthropic').

topics.description: Match docs-index entries by topic tag (e.g. 'mcp', 'agents', 'orchestration').

max_results.description: Maximum number of doc results to return.

tools.name: recommend_architecture
tools.title: Recommend Architecture
tools.description: The main user-facing architecture recommendation tool. Accepts a workflow goal and constraints, then returns a concrete, opinionated architecture recommendation with route, step classification, stack guidance, approval gates, do-not-build anti-patterns and confidence scoring. Uses deterministic graph routing — no LLM calls. Candidate routes are clearly labelled. High-risk workflows always include approval guidance. Use compose_workflow_route for raw route composition without architecture framing.

goal.description: Describe the AI workflow you want to build. Example: 'I want to scrape job listings, normalise the data and store it for search.'

current_stack.description: Technologies you are already using (e.g. ['nextjs', 'sqlite', 'openai']). Used to contextualise the stack recommendation.

preferred_models.description: LLM providers/models you prefer (e.g. ['openai', 'anthropic']). Informational — does not affect routing.

integrations.description: External services/integrations required (e.g. ['email', 'calendar', 'slack']). Used to surface approval gate requirements.

risk_level.description: Maximum acceptable risk level. High/critical inputs trigger mandatory approval gate guidance.

budget_preference.description: Cost preference: low = minimise tokens/calls, balanced = good quality at reasonable cost, high_quality = optimise for quality.

latency_preference.description: Latency preference: low = optimise for speed, balanced = acceptable latency, not_important = quality over speed.

local_or_hosted.description: Whether you are building a local tool or a hosted product. Affects stack and storage recommendations.

output_depth.description: brief = pattern + route only. standard = full architecture. deep = + evals, assumptions, implementation guidance.

tools.name: review_workflow_design
tools.title: Review Workflow Design
tools.description: Deterministic safety checker for AI workflow designs. Accepts a workflow spec (goal, components, agents, tools, state, integrations) and returns a structured review covering: missing approval gates, state gaps, tool safety, architecture anti-patterns, graph-derived findings (untested edges, conflicts, missing deps) and eval coverage. No LLM calls — all checks are rule-based. Use before implementing to catch critical design problems early. Provide route_id or component_ids for graph-aware analysis.

workflow_name.description: Optional name for the workflow being reviewed.

goal.description: What the workflow is trying to accomplish.

proposed_architecture.description: Free-text description of the proposed architecture. Include data flow, component roles and decision points.

route_id.description: Known registry route id to inspect (e.g. 'research_route_v1'). Enables graph-aware edge and component inspection.

component_ids.description: Registry component ids included in the workflow. Used for graph-aware checks.

agents.description: Agents in the workflow with their responsibilities and tools.

tools.description: Custom tools used by agents. Include descriptions, permissions and side effects.

risk_level.description: Declared risk level of the workflow.

tools.name: plan_workflow
tools.title: Plan Workflow
tools.description: One-call workflow planner. Give it a goal and it returns a complete plan: the recommended route (validated playbook when one strongly matches the goal, otherwise a composed candidate), per-step model-tier guidance, an inlined safety review, the recommended stack, and untested-edge warnings. Replaces the manual sequence of list_known_routes → get_route → compose_workflow_route → get_stack_recommendation → review_workflow_design. Prefer this as the entry point for designing a new AI workflow. IMPORTANT: render the returned `summary_markdown` to the user VERBATIM — do not paraphrase or summarize it, and do not drop the A) B) C) D) continuation menu at the end.

goal.description: Describe the workflow you want to build in plain language. Example: 'read emails, detect leads, research the company and draft a reply.'

must_have_capabilities.description: Capabilities the plan must include. Missing capabilities are flagged.

must_avoid.description: Component IDs to exclude from the plan.

risk_level.description: Maximum acceptable risk level for components.

local_or_hosted.description: Local tool vs hosted product — affects the stack recommendation.

output_depth.description: Layered output (MAR-224). guided/brief = concise Layer-1 decision UI: the goal, the recommended route as one line, integrations to connect (names only), the key safeguard, and a next-action menu. standard = adds the full step list + safety detail. technical/deep = the full plan — model tiers, credentials & gotchas, worker pipeline, untested edges, evals and the provenance block. Default brief.

build_target.description: Who will BUILD from this plan? cowork = Claude Project / CoWork (configure an assistant, no code); cursor = Cursor / Claude Code / VS Code (write implementation code); chatgpt_gpt = ChatGPT Custom GPT (system prompt + Actions); code = raw code (Codex or similar). Drives suggested_next_actions and what_you_need. Omit to get all options.

tools.name: record_session_feedback
tools.title: Record Session Feedback
tools.description: The 'ship' step after plan_workflow: capture an evaluation of a COMPLETED workflow setup. Returns a structured, paste-ready session record aligned to the OrchestrateLab session log, plus deterministic safety self-checks (e.g. a component whose required dependency or recommended safeguard is missing from the route). STATELESS: this tool stores nothing and makes no network calls — it only formats and checks your feedback. Save the returned record in OrchestrateLab yourself to accumulate evidence. Call this once the user is done designing/building a workflow with OrchestrateMCP.

goal.description: The agent goal this session designed (plain language).

route_components.description: Component ids that ended up in the shipped/used route (the completed setup).

route_selected.description: Route or playbook id/name that was used, if any.

edges_used.description: Edge ids used in the route.

untested_edges.description: Untested edges that shipped → edge validation queue.

ratings.description: Per-dimension 1-5 scores (MAR-122 rubric). modelOutputRating is their average.

overall_rating.description: Overall 1-5 rating (0 = unrated). Used when per-dimension ratings are absent.

missing_components.description: Capabilities you needed but the registry lacked → component gap queue.

playbook_candidate.description: Slug for a promotable golden-path playbook → promotion queue.

baseline_comparison.description: Optional A/B note vs. a build made WITHOUT the MCP.

tools.name: explain_component
tools.title: Explain Component
tools.description: Returns a plain-language, operator-friendly explanation of a single workflow component — what it does, what it needs, what it produces, which other steps it connects to, and what can go wrong. Intended for non-technical builders who are assembling an AI agent without deep technical knowledge. Use get_graph_component instead when you need structured JSON fields for programmatic processing or technical documentation.

component_id.description: The id of the component to explain (e.g. 'human_approval_gate', 'data_scraper').

tools.name: validate_playbook_candidate
tools.title: Validate Playbook Candidate
tools.description: Deterministic, read-only gate for the playbook factory. Give it a candidate playbook YAML and it checks the Definition of Done against the live registry — missing components, invalid edges, missing failure modes/evals, risk + approval policy, LLM-vs-deterministic separation, sources — and reports which lifecycle stage it qualifies for (draft → candidate → beta). It never writes to the registry and never certifies validated/published (those need Lab evidence it cannot see).

playbook_yaml.description: The candidate playbook as YAML text (same shape as registry/playbooks/*.playbook.yaml). Validated against the live registry and the Definition of Done.

tools.name: export_build_brief
tools.title: Export Build Brief
tools.description: Takes a plan_workflow result and emits a provenance-tagged Build Brief — a self-contained handoff document (§0 Constraints → §9 Observability wiring) ready to paste into an IDE agent prompt, a Linear issue, or an Obsidian note. Also emits a deterministic agent.manifest.json (agent_manifest) for DASH monitoring. Stateless: stores nothing, makes no network calls. Call after plan_workflow to get the agent-ready build spec.

goal.description: The original workflow goal — verbatim from plan_workflow.

evals_to_add.description: plan_workflow.evals_to_add — eval gaps the plan surfaced.

worker_pipeline.description: plan_workflow.worker_pipeline — build team (optional). Nullable since MAR-256: plan_workflow returns null at guided/brief/standard depth, not just omission — a straight pass-through of that field must not fail validation.

loop_guidance.description: plan_workflow.loop_guidance — null unless route is loop-shaped.

approval_gate_advisory.description: plan_workflow.approval_gate_advisory — non-null when gate is downgraded to advisory.

handoff_targets.description: Output formats to include: 'prompt' = paste-ready agent prompt, 'linear' = Linear issue description, 'obsidian' = Obsidian note. Defaults to prompt only.

playbook_id.description: plan_workflow.playbook.id when plan_source is 'playbook'; '' when composed.

route_id.description: plan_workflow.playbook.route_id when plan_source is 'playbook'; '' when composed.

build_target.description: Where the agent will be BUILT — recorded in the DASH manifest. Defaults to 'code'.

output_location.description: Where this agent's output lands (from the plan-time monitoring question), echoed into the DASH manifest. Free text, e.g. 'HubSpot notes + Gmail drafts'.

agent_name.description: Override the manifest agent slug. Defaults to a slug of the playbook id or goal.

tools.name: health_check
tools.title: Health Check
tools.description: Returns the server name, version, a summary of loaded registry entities (components, edges, stacks, routes, playbooks), and a build fingerprint. The `build.stale` field is true when the dist/ registry is outdated vs the source — run `pnpm build` to fix. The `safe_to_demo` field is true only when the build is fresh and the registry meets the published count floor; `demo_blockers` lists any reasons it is not. Use this to confirm the MCP server is running and the registry is fresh.
